EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low moved southeast of southern New England.  As this occurred, an inverted trough set up, bringing snow to much of the area.  Snow amounts were low (under 3 inches) for much of the area.  However, the higher elevations ended up with 6+ inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved out of the midwest, off the mid-Atlantic coast and northeastward across Nantucket and the outer arm of Cape Cod bringing accumulating snow to much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to nine inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm brought heavy snow and significant coastal flooding to the forecast area. This was an unusual synoptic set-up, with low pressure lingering off the coast of southern New England for several days. Snowfall was difficult to forecast due to concerns about precipitation type and boundary layer temperature. In the end, precipitation type turned out to be all snow for much of the area, with most locations receiving 1 to 2 feet of snow.  In addition, the Massachusetts east coast was hit by widespread moderate and pockets of major coastal flooding for two high tide cycles and beach erosion for at least 5 high tide cycles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to sixteen inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward across southern New England Sunday night (Oct 6) into Monday (Oct 7) creating a warm, moist, unstable atmosphere over the region.  Monday afternoon and evening (Oct 7) a strong cold front brought a line of strong to severe showers and storms through southern New England.  Strong, damaging winds occurred both with the storms and in the tight pressure gradient ahead of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree on Harbor Avenue was downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An historic winter storm deposited tremendous amounts of snow over all of southern New England, mainly from the mid-afternoon on Friday, February 8 and lasting into the daylight hours of Saturday, February 9. What made this an amazing storm was the widespread coverage of heavy snowfall.  Most locations received 2 to 2.5 feet of snow!  A stationary band of even heavier snowfall persisted from southwest NH through central MA and on to the southwest across central and western CT. In those areas, reports averaged closer to 2.5 to 3 feet! Isolated thunderstorms were common across the entire region during the height of the storm.||A low pressure system advancing from the Great Lakes region combined forces with a very moist low pressure system moving northeast from the Gulf Coast states. Explosive deepening took place Friday evening, February 8, as a low center moved from the North Carolina coast to south of Nantucket.  Strong high pressure to the north of New England helped ensure that cold air remained in place over the area. Snowfall gained intensity during the afternoon, but during the night, 2 to 3 inch per hour amounts were common throughout the region. The band of heaviest snowfall, with 3 to 5 inches per hour for several hours, extended from southwest NH to central and western CT.  Snow ended in the morning in western and central MA, southwest NH, most of CT and RI, and in the early afternoon across eastern MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixteen to twenty-four inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm moved across the southern New England coastline, spreading a wintry mix of precipitation across much of the area.  While the precipitation started as snow for most locations, snow quickly transitioned to sleet and freezing rain across portions of Connecticut, Rhode Island, and southeastern Massachusetts.  Areas of northern Massachusetts and southwestern New Hampshire remained all snow and received around a foot of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to fourteen inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong pressure rises behind a cold front coupled with cold air advection resulted in strong to damaging winds across much of southern New England. Most of the damage was to trees, some of which were still at least partially leaved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Manchester-Boston Regional Airport (KMHT) recorded a wind gust of 53 mph.  Higher gusts are estimated to have occurred elsewhere in eastern Hillsborough County.  In Nashua, a tree was downed at the intersection of East Dunstable Road and Shelley Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across Cape Cod bringing accumulating snow to much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to seven inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward across southern New England.  This brought a period of mainly rain and warm temperatures.  In addition, a strong low level jet (up to 80 kts) resulted in high winds across much of southern New England.  There was some tree damage and downed power lines with winds gusting to 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported sustained winds of 40 mph in Manchester.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Southern New England, resulting in strong cold air advection and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on Longview and North Roads in Hancock.  Trees and wires were downed on Union Street and on Greenfield Road in Peterborough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was the focus for thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall on the afternoon of June 30th. 1 to 3 inches of rain fell in less than 3 hours causing rapid rises on streams in Sullivan, Merrimack, and Belknap Counties. Numerous road washouts were reported in all three counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped 1 to 3 inches of rain in less than 3 hours in Claremont washing out 8 roads around town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was the focus for thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall on the afternoon of June 30th. 1 to 3 inches of rain fell in less than 3 hours causing rapid rises on streams in Sullivan, Merrimack, and Belknap Counties. Numerous road washouts were reported in all three counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 1 to 3 inches of rain producing flash flooding in Lakeport. Union Avenue was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across Cape Cod bringing accumulating snow to much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to eight inches of snow fell across western and central Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved through southern New England, triggering showers and thunderstorms across much of the area.  Some of these storms became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds.  Also precipitable water values were near two inches, allowing for heavy rain with all of these storms, which resulted in a few instances of poor drainage flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires on Honey Road in Marlow were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary across northern portions of the forecast area was the focus for afternoon convection on the afternoon of June 23rd. A weak shortwave swinging in from the west helped to increase wind fields aloft and strong heating south of the front produced CAPES in the 1000 to 1500 j/kg range. Hail and damaging winds were the main threats with these storms, the stronger of which held together long enough to drift into southern portions of the region later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near Milton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and cooling air in the upper levels resulted in steepening mid-level lapse rates, producing showers and thunderstorms. A warm, unstable environment allowed these storms to become severe, with several storms producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell in northern Manchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward through Southern New England, resulting in moist southerly flow across much of the area.  Showers and thunderstorms developed across western Massachusetts and southern New Hampshire, producing heavy rainfall towards the end of one of the wettest June's in southern New England history.  This resulted in flash flooding in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River Road in Westmoreland was flooded.  Route 12A was washed out and flooded between in multiple locations in Alstead and Surry, including between Gilsum Road and Webster Road in Surry.  Gilsum Mine Road was flooded and impassable in multiple locations in Alstead.  Route 123 was closed at Sargent Road in Marlow due to flooding and debris in the road.  Portions of North, Thayer Brook, and Walpole Valley Roads in Alstead were closed due to flooding.  Two brooks overflowed their banks along Sand Pond Road in Marlow.  The road and one house were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was the focus for thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall on the afternoon of June 30th. 1 to 3 inches of rain fell in less than 3 hours causing rapid rises on streams in Sullivan, Merrimack, and Belknap Counties. Numerous road washouts were reported in all three counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 1.5 to 3 inches of rain near New London sending several area streams over their banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal surface trough developed over southern New England, providing a focus for low level convection.  Plenty of instability, as well as a strong upper jet aloft, allowed several of these storms to become severe, producing damaging winds.  A very moist, tropical-like atmosphere resulted in very heavy rainfall which produced flash flooding, mainly across southwestern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck an attached garage on Reed Drive in Bedford, NH, starting a fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormal heat for early September coupled with an elevated mixed layer resulted in a few strong to severe thunderstorms developing late in the day in western Massachusetts and southwestern New Hampshire.  These storms produced damaging winds, downing several trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires on Pine Avenue were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of slow moving thunderstorms dropped 2 to 4 inches of rain in 4 hours in the Hooksett area of New Hampshire. This caused flooding and road washouts on several streets in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed behind an arctic cold front during the afternoon of the 24th.  Winds gusted to between 40 and 50 mph across much of New Hampshire.  Snapped trees and branches caused power outages throughout the region.  Power companies reported that about 30,000 customers lost electrical service.  In Laconia, one tree company worker was struck and killed by a tree as he was working to remove another tree from a roadway.  In Concord where winds gusted to 58 mph, a large fiber communications cable fell across I-93 blocking the interstate highway for three hours.
A cold front approaching from the west was the focus for afternoon convection. One cell moving through southeast New Hampshire became severe, producing large hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near Derry. Winds were also estimated at 60 mph with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west was the focus for afternoon convection. One cell moving through southeast New Hampshire became severe, producing large hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1.50 inch hail in Windham.

A cold front moving across Southern New England provided enough lift for showers and thunderstorms to develop in the warm, unstable environment ahead of the front. A few of these storms became severe resulting in both large hail and damaging winds. Up to one and a half inch diameter hail was reported as well as some tree damage.

Strong pressure rises behind a cold front coupled with cold air advection resulted in strong to damaging winds across much of southern New England. Most of the damage was to trees, some of which were still at least partially leaved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Silver Ranch Airport in Jaffrey (KAFN) recorded a wind gust of 43 mph.  An amateur radio operator recorded a wind gust of 50 mph on their home weather station in Rindge.  In addition, numerous damage reports were received, suggesting higher gusts over the highly variable terrain of Cheshire County.  Numerous trees and wires were downed throughout Hinsdale, Swanzey, Chesterfield, Alstead, Walpole, Harrisville, Keene, Dublin, Gilsum, Jaffrey, Rindge, Surry, and Fitzwilliam.  A couple of these trees were downed onto houses, one in Keene and another in Dublin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong pressure rises behind a cold front coupled with cold air advection resulted in strong to damaging winds across much of southern New England. Most of the damage was to trees, some of which were still at least partially leaved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and wires were downed throughout western and central Hillsborough County, including in: Temple, Peterborough, Greenfield, Lyndeborough, Francestown, and Pelham.  One of these trees was downed onto a house on Windham Road in Pelham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong low level jet coupled with strong pressure falls associated with a low pressure region approaching southern New England resulted in strong to damaging winds across southern New England. Damage was largely to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed onto Base Hill Road in Keene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure moved along a stationary front stretched across Southern New England.  This resulted in a few showers and thunderstorms which produced heavy rainfall and resulted in flash flooding in Cheshire County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Davis Street was flooded with a car stranded in its flood waters.  Ralston Street was flooded with three feet of water.  Parking lots off of Winchester Street were flooded with water up to the car doors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm moved across the southern New England coastline, spreading a wintry mix of precipitation across much of the area.  While the precipitation started as snow for most locations, snow quickly transitioned to sleet and freezing rain across portions of Connecticut, Rhode Island, and southeastern Massachusetts.  Areas of northern Massachusetts and southwestern New Hampshire remained all snow and received around a foot of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to sixteen inches of snow fell across western and central Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving east from the Ohio Valley intensified off the New Jersey coast during the morning of the 19th and then moved northeast to Nova Scotia by the morning of the 20th.  The developing low brought heavy snow to the entire State with close to a foot of snow in southeastern sections.  Across the western part of the state, from 6 to 10 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two days with high temperatures in the 50s and 1 to 2 inches of rain caused an ice jam on the Sugar River at West Claremont. Minor flooding occurred on Twistback Road and Beauregard Street.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt combined to cause an ice jam and minor flooding on the Sugar River at West Claremont (flood stage 7.0 ft), which crested at 10.03 ft. Water flooded Twistback Road and Beauregard Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving east from the Great Lakes and another low moving northeast along the East Coast brought heavy snow to most of central and eastern New Hampshire. Snowfall amounts ranged up to 6 to 14 inches across most of the area.  In southeastern New Hampshire, the rain mixed with snow and accumulations were less.  Western sections of the State generally saw 2 to 6 inches of snow.

Western sections of the State generally saw 2 to 6 inches of snow.

An area of low pressure moving northeast along the East Coast brought heavy snow to sections of Western Maine.  The heaviest snow fell in Sullivan, Carroll, and southern Coos Counties where 6 to 10 inches of snow fell.

An historic winter storm deposited tremendous amounts of snow over all of southern New England, mainly from the mid-afternoon on Friday, February 8 and lasting into the daylight hours of Saturday, February 9. What made this an amazing storm was the widespread coverage of heavy snowfall.  Most locations received 2 to 2.5 feet of snow!  A stationary band of even heavier snowfall persisted from southwest NH through central MA and on to the southwest across central and western CT. In those areas, reports averaged closer to 2.5 to 3 feet! Isolated thunderstorms were common across the entire region during the height of the storm.||A low pressure system advancing from the Great Lakes region combined forces with a very moist low pressure system moving northeast from the Gulf Coast states. Explosive deepening took place Friday evening, February 8, as a low center moved from the North Carolina coast to south of Nantucket.  Strong high pressure to the north of New England helped ensure that cold air remained in place over the area. Snowfall gained intensity during the afternoon, but during the night, 2 to 3 inch per hour amounts were common throughout the region. The band of heaviest snowfall, with 3 to 5 inches per hour for several hours, extended from southwest NH to central and western CT.  Snow ended in the morning in western and central MA, southwest NH, most of CT and RI, and in the early afternoon across eastern MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fourteen to twenty-seven inches of snow fell across Cheshire County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low moved southeast of southern New England.  As this occurred, an inverted trough set up, bringing snow to much of the area.  Snow amounts were low (under 3 inches) for much of the area.  However, the higher elevations ended up with 6+ inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five inches of snow fell across Cheshire County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure moved off the Virginia coast on the morning of the 6th and then moved very slowly northeast during the next 60 hours.  A trough extending northwest from the low brushed southeastern New Hampshire with heavy snow.  Snowfall amounts in Rockingham County ranged from 4 to 12 inches with the higher amounts in southern sections.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm brought heavy snow and significant coastal flooding to the forecast area. This was an unusual synoptic set-up, with low pressure lingering off the coast of southern New England for several days. Snowfall was difficult to forecast due to concerns about precipitation type and boundary layer temperature. In the end, precipitation type turned out to be all snow for much of the area, with most locations receiving 1 to 2 feet of snow.  In addition, the Massachusetts east coast was hit by widespread moderate and pockets of major coastal flooding for two high tide cycles and beach erosion for at least 5 high tide cycles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to eleven inches of snow fell across Cheshire County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two days with high temperatures in the 50s and 1 to 2 inches of rain caused an ice jam on the Sugar River at West Claremont. Minor flooding occurred on Twistback Road and Beauregard Street.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam caused minor flooding on the Sugar River in West Claremont (flood stage 7.0 ft), which crested at 10.3 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking along a slow moving cold front produced 1.5 to 3 inches of rain across northern and central New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flooding on the Androscoggin River at Gorham (flood stage 8.0 ft) which crested at 8.46 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and an associated cold front approached the forecast area on the afternoon of June 17th. Moderate to strong speed shear shifted into southern New Hampshire by mid afternoon as CAPE values peaked. Several severe thunderstorms developed during the remainder of the afternoon with wind damage the main feature with these storms. Some small hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed several large trees near Henniker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 30th of June a stalled frontal boundary remained in place to the northwest of the forecast area. To the south of the front, hot and very humid air prevailed across the region. Very high precipitable water values also persisted across the region.  Afternoon heating produce slow moving thunderstorms which dropped very heavy rainfall and produced several reports of thunderstorm wind damage due to wet microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Jobs Creek Road in Sunapee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The highest astronomical tides of the month combined with a weak, but developing, area of low pressure (1000 mb) near Cape Cod to produce coastal flooding in coastal Rockingham County during the late evening hours of May 25th. Fortunately winds were relatively light, with relatively modest seas along the coast. Flooding occurred on several of the lower back roads in Hampton, including Brown Avenue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with moderate seas and developing low pressure to produce coastal flooding in coastal Rockingham County. Flooding occurred on several of the lower back roads in Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front combined with strong wind fields and high CAPES over the region resulting in a widespread severe thunderstorm outbreak during the afternoon and evening hours of June 2nd. These storms produced mainly wind damage with isolated reports of large hail and some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Lebanon and West Lebanon blocking Route 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through the region during the day on the 19th of July, leaving the forecast area in a very hot and humid air mass. Thunderstorms riding over an upper level ridge along this boundary developed during the late afternoon across eastern Ontario and southern Quebec. These storm intensified as they dropped southeast through the evening hours. Damage from these storms was exclusively due to strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires in Wakefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place over the region on the 2nd of July. Precipitable water values approaching 2 inches were common across the forecast area. Thunderstorms which formed on this day produced very heavy rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in less than 3 hours over already saturated ground. This resulted in the destruction of many roads and the flooding of apartments and residences in both Sullivan and Grafton counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from slow moving thunderstorms produced flash flooding in Cornish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave and associated cold front were approaching from the northwest during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 17th. Very high precipitable water values and very warm temperatures ahead of the front combined with increasing shear to produce numerous severe thunderstorms, mainly during the evening hours. Due to high freezing levels, wind damage and very heavy rain were the main features of these storms as they moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees throughout Lyman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal surface trough developed over southern New England, providing a focus for low level convection.  Plenty of instability, as well as a strong upper jet aloft, allowed several of these storms to become severe, producing damaging winds.  A very moist, tropical-like atmosphere resulted in very heavy rainfall which produced flash flooding, mainly across southwestern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires on South Bennington Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.  A tree on Greenfield Road was downed onto a house.  A tree and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds.

These storm intensified as they dropped southeast through the evening hours. Damage from these storms was exclusively due to strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees along Route 3 from Groveton into Colebrook and further north into Pittsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal surface trough developed over southern New England, providing a focus for low level convection.  Plenty of instability, as well as a strong upper jet aloft, allowed several of these storms to become severe, producing damaging winds.  A very moist, tropical-like atmosphere resulted in very heavy rainfall which produced flash flooding, mainly across southwestern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flash flooding across much of Cheshire County.  Four to six inches of rain fell in just a few hours across parts of the area.  In Keene, water ten inches deep flooded Church and Roxbury Streets.  The basement of city hall was flooded.  Six inches of water flooded Blake Street.  Eastern Avenue and Washington Street were flooded and impassable, with at least one flooded basement.  A portion of Woodland Avenue washed out.  Flooded basements were reported along Martin and Beech Streets.  Water, Concord, Bellevue, Sullivan, Rule, Roxbury, and North Lincoln Streets as well as Woodland and Eastern Avenues, Old Concord Road, and Roxbury Road were all damaged by the flooding.  In Swanzey, Homestead Avenue and Swanzey Factory Road were flooded and impassable.  A house along Homestead Avenue had a flooded basement.  Wheelock Brook overflowed its banks onto Cobble Hill Road.  In Sullivan, Otter Brook overflowed its banks onto the Centre Street bridge, depositing mud and other debris onto the bridge.  In Harrisville, Chesham Road was washed out near the intersection with Old Chesham Road.  In Jaffrey, a basement was flooded along Pine Street.  North Street was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place over the forecast area on the afternoon of June 24th. Convection developed early in the afternoon over eastern New York and western New England and shifted east into the favorable environment across New Hampshire and western Maine through the evening hours. Very weak shear and high precipitable water resulted in pulse type convection with large hail and wet microbursts the main threats. Thirty people were injured by lightning at a Boy Scout Camp near Gilmanton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike injured 29 scouts and 1 leader at Camp Bell Boy Scout Camp on Griswold Scout Reservation near Gilmanton.  All the Scouts were transported to a local hospital and were treated for burns, ranging from minor to serious.  Several of the scouts were retained overnight for observation and treatment.  The Scouts had taken shelter under a tarp strung between trees when lightning struck a tall pine tree about 30 feet away.  The current traveled through the ground to the scouts who were sitting around a table on metal chairs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and an associated cold front approached the forecast area on the afternoon of June 17th. Moderate to strong speed shear shifted into southern New Hampshire by mid afternoon as CAPE values peaked. Several severe thunderstorms developed during the remainder of the afternoon with wind damage the main feature with these storms. Some small hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree on a car on Islington Street in Portsmouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary across northern portions of the forecast area was the focus for afternoon convection on the afternoon of June 23rd. A weak shortwave swinging in from the west helped to increase wind fields aloft and strong heating south of the front produced CAPES in the 1000 to 1500 j/kg range. Hail and damaging winds were the main threats with these storms, the stronger of which held together long enough to drift into southern portions of the region later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near Mount Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm located over the Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 14th moved northeast during the day.  A secondary low developed off the Mid-Atlantic coast and intensified rapidly by the morning of the 15th.  The secondary low continued to intensify as it moved through the Gulf of Maine during the morning of the 15th and into the Canadian Maritimes by the morning of the 16th.  The low spread heavy snow across all of New Hampshire with amounts generally ranging from 8 to 14 inches.

A warm front moved northward across southern New England.  This brought a period of mainly rain and warm temperatures.  In addition, a strong low level jet (up to 80 kts) resulted in high winds across much of southern New England.  There was some tree damage and downed power lines with winds gusting to 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operators in Richmond and Keene recorded wind gusts of 48 and 33 mph respectively, on their home weather stations.  A tree and wires were downed on East Lake Road in Fitzwilliam.

An area of low pressure over the southeastern states on the morning of the 29th moved rapidly north-northeastward to Nova Scotia by the morning of the 30th.  The low brought abundant moisture northward and produced 5 to 10 inches of snow across sections of central New Hampshire.  In southern and coastal sections of the state, warmer temperatures caused the precipitation to change to rain.

Low pressure developed in the Mid-Atlantic States during the day on the 17th and spread snow northeastward during the afternoon and evening as it moved into the Gulf of Maine.  Southeast New Hampshire generally saw between 4 and 8 inches of snow from the storm.

Low pressure tracking along the southern coast of New England brought snow to much of the interior of Massachusetts and southwestern New Hampshire. Closer to the low (closer to the coasts) snow changed over to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to seven inches of snow fell across Cheshire County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed rapidly off the Carolina coast late on the 7th and early on the 8th.  The storm moved very slowly northeast during the 8th and 9th as it continued to intensify.  By the morning of the 10th, the storm was located just to the east of Nova Scotia.  The storm brought heavy snow, high winds, and blizzard conditions to the southeastern part of the state.  Snowfall amounts were generally 18 inches or more in the southeast where blizzard conditions caused considerable blowing and drifting snow.  In western and northern sections, snowfall amounts were in the 4 to 18 inch range.  Southeastern New Hampshire had blizzard conditions for about 3 to 10 hours.

Strong winds developed behind an arctic cold front during the afternoon of the 24th.  Winds gusted to between 40 and 50 mph across much of New Hampshire.  Snapped trees and branches caused power outages throughout the region.  Power companies reported that about 30,000 customers lost electrical service.  In Laconia, one tree company worker was struck and killed by a tree as he was working to remove another tree from a roadway.  In Concord where winds gusted to 58 mph, a large fiber communications cable fell across I-93 blocking the interstate highway for three hours.

A warm front lifted north through the region during the morning and early afternoon of October 7th. By mid afternoon much of New Hampshire had broken into the warm sector with temperatures pushing into the mid 70s. A strong cold front approaching from the west set the stage for late afternoon convection in a highly sheared environment. Damage associated with these storms was exclusively due to strong winds as a squall line with bowing segments moved through the region during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees and wires in Lancaster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward across southern New England Sunday night (Oct 6) into Monday (Oct 7) creating a warm, moist, unstable atmosphere over the region.  Monday afternoon and evening (Oct 7) a strong cold front brought a line of strong to severe showers and storms through southern New England.  Strong, damaging winds occurred both with the storms and in the tight pressure gradient ahead of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds on Streeter Hill Road in Chesterfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front stalled just north of Southern New England resulted in a warm, unstable, moist airmass across southern New Hampshire.  Plenty of instability and a 30-40 knot low level jet resulted in showers and thunderstorms while preciptable water values above 2 inches resulted in heavy rain and subsequently, flash flooding in Cheshire County.  The Governor of New Hampshire declared a state of emergency on July 3 for flooding in Cheshire County as well as Grafton and Sullivan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of rain fell across Cheshire County, some locations receiving that much in one hour.  This resulted in widespread flooding.  In Westmoreland, numerous roads were flooded and many were closed including Route 63 from South Village Road to Route 12, Route 12 from Route 63 to the Keene line, Depot Road, Eagle Road, London Cross Road near Route 12, and areas along Route 61.  The Mill brook overflowed its banks at the intersection of Route 12 and Aldrich Road.  Up to one foot of water and debris flooded the road.  Twenty-five to thirty campers were evacuated from the campground on Route 63 due to flooding.  Part of Route 12 was washed out in the South Village area of Westmoreland.  One lane of Route 63 at Chickering Road was washed out.  Part of Cross Road also was washed out.  The River Road north bridge near the Walpole town line was washed away.  Emergency personnel evacuated parts of the South Village area of Westmoreland.  In Walpole, the intersection of Watkins Hill and County Roads was flooded with over a foot of water.  North Road was closed at Matt Brook.  Several basements were flooded in Gilsum on Vessel Rock Road and Route 10.  Part of North Road in Alstead was closed due to flooding.  Other roads that were closed are Route 123 from Marlow Village to the Alstead line in Marlow, Route 12A from Carpenter to Walpole Valley Road in Surry, and Route 123 at Gilsum Mine Road in Alstead.  In Alstead, Thayer Brook Road near Corbin Road, Route 12A north of Walpole Valley Road, Route 123 from Gilsum Mine Road to the Marlow town line, and South Woods Road were all closed.  A state of emergency was declared for Cheshire county because of the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and very humid airmass was in place across the region on the afternoon of August 28th. There was little in the way of shear and upper dynamics so storms which formed were very slow moving and produced very heavy rain in a short period of time. A thunderstorm in the Lebanon area produced 3 inches of rain in 1 hour causing flooding and road washouts. Several cars were stranded on Route 12A in West Lebanon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced 3 inches of rain in one hour in the Lebanon area of Grafton County, resulting in street flooding and road washouts. High water stranded several cars on Route 12A in West Lebanon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front dropping south through the region was the focus for afternoon and evening convection. High precipitable water values combined with weak shear to produced slow moving thunderstorms and very heavy rain. 3.75 inches of rain in one hour resulted road flooding and washouts in the Alexandria and Bridgewater areas of Grafton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced 3.75 inches of rain in one hour in Alexandria resulting in road flooding and washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front dropping south through the region was the focus for afternoon and evening convection. High precipitable water values combined with weak shear to produced slow moving thunderstorms and very heavy rain. 3.75 inches of rain in one hour resulted road flooding and washouts in the Alexandria and Bridgewater areas of Grafton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced 3.75 inches of rain in one hour in Bridgewater causing road flooding and washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong low pressure region (976mb) moved from Ontario into southern Quebec.  Southern New England was sandwiched in between this and high pressure off the coast to the southeast, creating a very tight pressure gradient over the region.  This resulted in strong gusty winds which caused some damage, especially to areas where the trees were still fully leaved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed onto Route 123 in Peterborough by strong winds.  Primary wires in Sharon were also downed by these winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong low pressure region (976mb) moved from Ontario into southern Quebec.  Southern New England was sandwiched in between this and high pressure off the coast to the southeast, creating a very tight pressure gradient over the region.  This resulted in strong gusty winds which caused some damage, especially to areas where the trees were still fully leaved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Jaffrey Municipal/Silver Ranch Airport (KAFN) recorded sustained wind speeds of 26 mph and gusts to 46 mph.  A handful of trees and wires were downed across Cheshire County, including in Harrisville, Nelson, Winchester, and Jaffrey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front combined with strong wind fields and high CAPES over the region resulting in a widespread severe thunderstorm outbreak during the afternoon and evening hours of June 2nd. These storms produced mainly wind damage with isolated reports of large hail and some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Colebrook and Errol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal surface trough developed over southern New England, providing a focus for low level convection.  Plenty of instability, as well as a strong upper jet aloft, allowed several of these storms to become severe, producing damaging winds.  A very moist, tropical-like atmosphere resulted in very heavy rainfall which produced flash flooding, mainly across southwestern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires on Spring Street were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across the Seacoast area dropping 1 to 3 inches of rain in less than three hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused flash flooding along Route 107 in Kennsington and along Route 1a in Hampton Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid air mass remained entrenched across the forecast area on the 20th of July. An approaching cold front set the stage for more severe thunderstorms across the region. Once again elevated freezing levels inhibited development of large hail and all reports of severe weather involved wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed large branches leading to power outages in Milan.
